restaurants.name: Ledbury, The
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: Australian chef Brett Graham’s Notting Hill restaurant reopened in February 2022 after a brief pandemic hiatus. The new-look Ledbury is more than ever before an expression of Graham’s passion for produce. Beef and Iberian pork come from his own animals; venison from the deer parks he operates (Aynhoe Park and Boughton House); and mushrooms from the state-of-the-art, moisture-controlled cabinet at the restaurant itself. New dishes reveal themselves with the changing seasons – for example, Poole Bay mackerel, blood orange, myoga ginger and coriander capers or Jersey beef, white asparagus, XO morel and kosho sauce. The impressive global wine list leans Francophile.

restaurants.name: Fortitude Bakehouse
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: You’ll have to stand in line to get your hands on a hot sausage roll or cream beignet from Irish baker Dee Rettali’s hugely popular bakery, tucked away down a quiet cobbled mews in Bloomsbury. Rettali is the former head of food at the late lamented Fernandez and Wells, and she specializes in sourdough cakes and bakes that keep time with the seasons. In winter, it might be ginger cream beignets and mince pies; in summer, blackberry Danish with honey cream or strawberry and frangipane galettes. It’s a spartan setup; seating is limited to a couple of benches outside.

restaurants.name: Mountain
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: Anglesey-born Tomos Parry is one of the hottest chefs on the London scene. Brat in Shoreditch is as busy as it was on its launch five years ago, and his 2013–2015 pop-up has triumphantly returned as Brat at Climpson’s Arch. Mountain, Parry’s first foray into Soho, features Basque cooking using Welsh ingredients. Menai oysters; spider crab omelette; lobster caldereta for four or five people; mutton chops, whole mallard duck or aged Friesian beef rib—all cooked on the wood grill. The sourcing is impeccable, the butchery in-house. Plan ahead, or try early for a walk-in at the counter.

restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.address: The Ritz London, 150 Piccadilly, London, United Kingdom
restaurants.description: In an era where most chefs do their best to make French cuisine as light as possible, John Williams focuses on tradition. With Escoffier being his hero (apropos in the location where the famed chef once ran the brigade),, the decadent French-inspired, Britain-honed cuisine he serves at the famous Grade II-listed hotel is a worthy tribute. Williams, who was plucked from Claridge’s in 1998, serves organic Cornish beef Wellington smothered in truffle and hay-baked Bresse chicken, while no meal is complete without a pan of crêpes Suzette sent up in buttery, alcoholic flame at your table.

restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.address: Pop Brixton, 19 Prebend St, London, United Kingdom
restaurants.description: Pophams Bakery’s photogenic rosemary and sea-salt twists and bacon and maple scrolls have been catnip to influencers since the beginning (2017). Since then, Pophams has grown to three sites (Islington, Hackney and Arcade Food Theatre) and has, at its Hackney site, expanded into pasta (evenings only). The viennoiserie is precisely crafted with three batons of chocolate in the pain au chocolat and a full 27 layers of butter in each pastry. The Marmite, Schlossberger and spring-onion pastry is a study in umami.

restaurants.name: Bleecker Burgers
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.address: Old Spitalfields Market, Lamb St, London, United Kingdom
restaurants.description: Former corporate lawyer Zan Kaufman launched Bleecker St. Burger in 2012 as a street-food concept, just as street food was taking off in London. Now there are four permanent sites, serving six kinds of burger – although the simple cheeseburger is the connoisseur's choice. Beef is rare-breed and pasture-fed from small UK farms; it is also dry-aged, making for a particularly intense beefiness. The angry fries (covered in hot sauce and blue cheese) are also a cult staple of the London chips scene.

restaurants.name: Gelupo
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: There’s always room for one more dessert, hence the round-the-clock appeal of Gelupo, Jacob Kennedy’s artisan gelateria opposite his Soho restaurant Bocca di Lupo. Ice cream aficionados will note the seasonal ingredients (prepared fresh daily), the classic flavors, and the reassuring lidded stainless steel carapine for correct storage. Straciatella, pistachio, ricotta and sour cherry are all top-notch, but nothing beats Gelupo’s hazelnut. Certain flavors like Panettone and pandoro are only available in season.

restaurants.name: Core by Clare Smyth
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.address: 92 Kensington Park Rd, London, United Kingdom
restaurants.description: Former Restaurant Gordon Ramsay chef patron Clare Smyth redefines luxury at her elegant Notting Hill debut restaurant. Though she eschews tablecloths and thick carpets, Smyth has not relaxed standards. Her two tasting menus – seven courses plus a parade of amuses – approach British produce anew, assuring a place at the table for such humble ingredients as potatoes (baked and laced with dulse beurre blanc) and carrots (lamb carrot is Smyth’s plant-forward signature) alongside more straightforwardly upscale Cornish brill, oysters and caviar.

restaurants.name: Ikoyi
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.address: 180 Strand, Temple, London WC2R 1EA, United Kingdom
restaurants.description: Ikoyi is one of London’s most extraordinary restaurants. Jeremy Chan acknowledges the West African culinary traditions that his kitchen moves within, using ingredients, spicing and techniques, but he does so in the context of fine dining, making a powerful argument against the dearth of African cuisines in the sphere. A smoked crab jollof rice and a plantain dusted with lurid raspberry salt and dipped in a scotch-bonnet mayonnaise are singular dishes that rely on the intersection of respectful history, impeccable technique and dayboat seafood or grass-fed meat from some of Britain’s finest producers.
photoCredits: Photo: Ikoyi image by Justin Hurst
Photo: Ikoyi image by Ekaterina Pugacheva

restaurants.name: Planque
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: London’s coolest oenophiles store and drink their wine at Planque, a self-described wine drinkers’ clubhouse under the arches in achingly hip Haggerston. The club’s members are gracious enough to host a public-facing restaurant with a star at the pass in Seb Myers (Auberge de Chassignolles, P. Franco, etc.). The food is French-leaning, seasonal and always surprising. Veal sweetbreads and braised Piattone beans or Anjou pigeon with black walnut and treviso are crying out to be matched with a bottle from the ever-evolving list of fine and off-the-wall, mainly French wines. Monthly guest collaborations, workshops, wine dinners and tastings bring new ideas.

restaurants.name: Kanada-Ya
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: The original Japanese Kanada-Ya was founded in Fukuoka Prefecture, the spiritual home of tonkotsu ramen, in 2008 by former pro keirin racer Kazuhiro Kanada. In 2014, Kanada-san brought it to London, opening the first site near Covent Garden. Kanada-Ya loyalists swear by the classic ramen with wood ear fungus, homemade noodles (available in gradations from soft to extra hard), 18-hour pork-bone broth and an optional chashu Burford Brown egg. The truffle version has its devotees too.

restaurants.name: Kitchen Table
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: This 20-seat counter restaurant in Fitzrovia features the cooking of the talented James Knappett. Working from an open kitchen. Knappett, whose CV includes stints with Thomas Keller and Marcus Waring, serves hyper-seasonal tasting menus only, with each course denoted by a single word: crab, or seaweed, or pigeon, for example, all sustainably sourced or foraged. James also offers a vegetarian menu. His wife, Sandia Chang, is the sommelier, and she has organized a wonderful list of wines that includes an exceptional selection of grower champagnes.

restaurants.name: Hélène Darroze at the Connaught
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.address: The Connaught, Carlos Pl, London, United Kingdom
restaurants.description: Fourth-generation chef Hélène Darroze splits her time between her restaurants in Paris and London, where she’s led The Connaught’s cosseting, wood-paneled flagship restaurant since 2008. Stellar ingredients – many on display under glass cloches – are her starting point across five-course, seven-course and “Inspiration” menus, where Cornish lobster, A5 wagyu from Gunma and milk-fed Basque lamb keep company with espelette pepper and Dupérier foie gras from her native Landes. The wine list is near-encyclopedic.

restaurants.name: Dorian
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: “A bistro for locals” is the tagline of this Parisian-style Notting Hill newcomer with its green banquettes, marble tables, and checkerboard tiles. That may sound exclusive, Notting Hill being about the toniest London postcode, but what owner Chris D’Sylva (Notting Hill Fish + Meat, Supermarket of Dreams) means is that it’s for regulars, not destination diners. Chef Max Coen (ex-Ikoyi, Kitchen Table, Frantzén) is a rare talent. He cooks bistro food in a modern British idiom using very fine produce, such as turbot with Porthilly oysters and wild garlic; liver parfait and quince soldiers; and deep-fried quail with hot sauce.

restaurants.name: Roti King
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: Kalpana Sugendran Sugendran, aka the Roti King, has been slinging roti since he was 14 years old. He came to the UK from Malaysia in 2003 and finally opened his first bricks-and-mortar in 2014 in Euston. The original site is still going strong, serving foodies, students, and travelers passing through the train station nearby, and there are now further branches in Waterloo and Battersea Power Station. Most come for the roti canai, freshly made before their eyes, but other Malaysian and Singaporean standards are available too, including char kuey teow and nasi lemak. A lively, low-budget address.

restaurants.name: The Mayfair Chippy
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.address: 14 N Audley St, London, United Kingdom
restaurants.description: The setting may be swanky (North Audley Street, no less), but the Mayfair Chippy stays mercifully free of airs and graces. Its stock in trade is traditional battered fish and chips – cod or haddock – with mushy peas, chip-shop curry sauce and wallies (large, pickled gherkins), and it even gives out free “scraps” (those yummy bits of leftover batter), just like they do up north. That said, Maldon oysters, truffle-loaded chips or lobster mac and cheese are there if you want them. Open seven days a week and as popular with locals as with tourists.

restaurants.name: Da Terra
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: Italo-Brazilian chef Rafael Cagali debuted Da Terra at Bethnal Green’s Town Hall Hotel in 2019, in the space formerly occupied by Lee Westcott’s Typing Room and, before that, Nuno Mendes’ Viajante. Cagali is an ambitious chef with a résumé boasting big names only (Quique Dacosta, The Fat Duck, Aulis…). His riff on the cooking of his homeland is as characterful as the abstract art hung in the room with highlights of the lengthy tasting menu, including cannelloni stuffed with courgette, trout and chervil, moqueca with aged turbot and cachaça or Hereford short rib with cassava, artichoke and chimichurri.

restaurants.name: Alain Ducasse at the Dorchester
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.address: The Dorchester, 53 Park Ln, London, United Kingdom
restaurants.description: Nice-born chef Jean-Philippe Blondet, an Alain Ducasse acolyte of some 15 years, has led the kitchen at his master’s elegant Mayfair outpost since 2016. His style is perfectly on-brand, an impressive coupling of fine French technique and exquisite ingredients such as Poulet Jaune des Landes, Périgord truffles, Scottish langoustines and Colston Bassett Stilton. Don’t miss the rum-drenched baba “comme à Monte Carlo” with Chantilly cream. The 1,000-bin wine list is unapologetically Francophile, with aristocratic bottles producers like Leroy and Romanée-Conti.

restaurants.name: Alex Dilling at Cafe Royal
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.address: Hotel Cafe Royal, 10 Air Street, London, United Kingdom
restaurants.description: This is the debut restaurant of chef Alex Dilling who forged his reputation in the UK at The Greenhouse and Hélène Darroze, and in New York at Caviar Russe and Alain Ducasse’s Adour. His tastefully appointed dining room within Hotel Café Royal upholds French haute cuisine traditions with unabashedly extravagant menus that are rich in foie gras, Scottish lobster, white truffles and his beloved caviar. Clam chowder, with Dorset clams, confit potato and Sarawak pepper, is a signature. The wine list nearly hits five digits at the pointy end.
photoCredits: Photo: Alex Dilling at Cafe Royal

restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.address: Shoreditch Town Hall, 380 Old St, London, United Kingdom
restaurants.description: Scottish chef Isaac McHale’s ambitious restaurant at Shoreditch Town Hall grew out of a supper club in a pub. Now one of London’s leading fine-dining destinations, it’s a ticketed, tasting menu–only experience with a serious yet unstuffy vibe. McHale (ex Ledbury and Noma) champions pristine British ingredients such as mullet (from esteemed supplier Kernowsashimi) with blood orange, fennel and mackerel garum, and spider crab (deviled in tarts) with elderflower hollandaise. Menus can be paired with wine, soft drink or tea flights.

restaurants.name: Gymkhana
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: The jewel in the crown of the Sethi family’s restaurant empire (see Hoppers, Brigadiers, etc.), Gymkhana draws inspiration from colonial India’s high-society sports clubs. Game and “secondary” cuts are the kitchen’s hallmark. The kid-goat methi keema with buttered buns and brains has been on since day one. Also not to be missed: wild muntjac biryani and the partridge pepper fry with malabar paratha. Beverages are in keeping with the theme – punches, lassis, G&Ts and quinine sours.

restaurants.name: KOL
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: The spotlight’s been on young Mexican star Santiago Lastra ever since René Redzepi picked him to lead Noma’s Tulum pop-up in 2017. Now ensconced in his own restaurant KOL, est. Marylebone 2020, Lastra takes British ingredients and makes them Mexican. Who else would pair langoustine tacos with sea buckthorn, short rib costillas with fermented blackcurrant, barbacoa of lamb with nettle and arbol salsa or buñuelos with Tunworth ice cream? On offer is an astounding list of Mexican spirits – tequila, mezcal, agave – complimented by a list of wines from central and eastern Europe.

restaurants.name: Jolene
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: Jolene, with branches in Islington, Shoreditch, and Newington Green, is the bakery arm of the restaurant group behind north London neighbourhood favorites Primeur and Westerns Laundry. Newington Green is the big one, as not only is it a bakery, selling such goodies as cardamom twists and forced rhubarb Danish pastries, it’s also a chic, zinc-barred restaurant with good looks straight out of Kinfolk magazine. The daily chalkboard is as seasonal as you’d want, with spätzle and wild mushrooms in the colder months, cucumber and almond ajo blanco or nectarine, tomato, and pecorino in the summer. Natural wines line the shelves.

restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: E5 Bakehouse is a busy, bustling bakery and café in Hackney and then some. Having started selling loaves in 2010, it’s developed into a progressive, ethically minded business with its own off-grid farm growing heritage cereals, an on-site mill and a refugee-training programme. Pop by for a loaf of Hackney Wild sourdough, pain au chocolat, a rhubarb and custard galette, or a rustic lunch of soup and a toastie with a glass of natty wine.

restaurants.name: Sketch Lecture Room
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: Sketch, restaurateur Mourad Mazouz and chef Pierre Gagnaire’s opulent pleasure palace in what was once Christian Dior’s Mayfair salon, celebrated its 20-year anniversary in 2023. The interiors set the tone – all sumptuous velvet and plush, patterned carpet – and the cuisine matches it. Head chef Daniel Stucki sends out waves of complex, multi-component preparations based on fine ingredients from near and far. Perhaps native lobster with black rice galette and coastal herbs or Limousin veal rack with savory and nigella cooked en cocotte before the legendary Grand Dessert. The wine list, a heavyweight tome, can hit five figures at the top end.

restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: Ever since the first Barrafina opened in Soho in 2007, long lines have snaked from its doors. The no-reservations restaurant, which now has five sites in London, is still turning out some of the best tapas and Spanish food in the city. Barcelona native Angel Zapata Martin took over as chef from founder Nieves Barragan Mohacho in 2017, and the offerings, like golden, oozing tortillas, pan con tomate or lemon sole with chorizo and pluma iberica, are all prepared in an open kitchen, with guests looking on while sipping their copas of sherry.

restaurants.description: Simon Rogan’s Soho chef’s table Aulis reopened in the summer of 2023 after a plush new look that saw it add a lounge and upsize from 8 seats to 12. Executive chef Oli Marlow has the pick of produce from Rogan’s Our Farm in Cartmel, just down the road from L’Enclume, which he shows off in meticulously executed dishes of Orkney scallops, wild chamomile, buttermilk and smoked pike perch; truffle pudding caramelized in birch, fermented black garlic and summer truffle; and cherries and raw milk ice cream. restaurants.description: Fergus Henderson kick-started the concept of nose-to-tail eating when he opened St. John in 1994, minimizing food waste by serving all parts of the animal. Henderson, who opened Soho’s The French House with his wife, Margot, continues to champion offal at his stark industrial Smithfield restaurant with dishes like grilled ox heart, pheasant and trotter pie and their famous bone marrow and parsley salad. Less offal-y dishes are also available. One of the most important restaurants to open in the past 30 years, it has influenced countless other restaurants all over the world.

restaurants.description: Sister restaurant to the popular Palomar, this horseshoe-shaped, counter restaurant in the heart of Covent Garden celebrates the food of Africa’s Barbary Coast. The menu offers nods to Algeria, Tunisia, Libya, Morocco and Israel, serving up some of London’s most compelling food in the process. There is a focus on seafood, including dishes like octopus mashawsha served with hot, garlic-laced chickpeas. A meal here is highly coveted and walk-in only, so plan for a wait if you arrive in the evening or on the weekend.

restaurants.description: Chutney Mary and sister restaurants Veeraswamy and Amaya make up a gilded trio of addresses that have redefined Indian fine dining in the capital. Chutney Mary started life in Chelsea in 1990, moving to opulent (to say the least) surrounds in St. James’s in 2015. The kitchen has expensive tastes: tandoori Dover sole, Madagascan prawns and griddled scallops in Mangalorean sauce are a lavish counterpoint to chicken tikka, Hyderabadi lamb shank and biryani. Finish with a clever ‘gulab jamun tiramisu’ hybrid.

restaurants.description: Fat Duck maestro Heston Blumenthal is listed as “creator” at Dinner by Heston Blumenthal at the Mandarin Oriental Hyde Park. Executing the maestro’s vision since May ’23 is head chef Adam Tooby-Desmond, who’s worked his way up the ranks. The menu is inspired by historic gastronomy, best evidenced by the iconic Meat Fruit from c.1500, smoked chicken and salsify Salamagundy from c.1723 and Tipsy Cake c.1858 with spit-roast pineapple. The Hereford rib eye and triple-cooked chips hark back to c.1830, apparently. You can order Tignanello or Silex by the glass or a special bottle from the world’s most famous vineyards.

restaurants.description: It has been 20 years since Bilbao native Nieves Barragan Mohacho took a job as the sous chef at Fino, the predecessor of the popular Barrafina group of restaurants. By 2007, she had risen to the position of head chef, and she spent the next ten years expanding the restaurant to four locations. In 2017, she opened a restaurant of her own, where a variety of tapas is served at the downstairs counter, while at the upstairs asador, she serves whole Segovia sucking pigs and Galician beef ribs that are cooked in a traditional wood-fired oven.

restaurants.description: The question of where to find London’s best burger is open to debate, but Patty and Bun (est. 2012) is one of the small chains whose name you’ll hear bandied about. The question of Patty and Bun’s best burger is also open to debate. Some say it’s the Ari Gold, the dry-aged patty dressed with smoky mayo and pickled onions; others say it’s the Smokey Robinson, with caramelized onions and bacon. Either way, expect dry-aged beef from H. G. Walter, a soft brioche bun and tasty, hand-cut, rosemary-salted fries. Reservations taken.

restaurants.description: The follow-up to ex-Ottolenghi chef Josh Katz’s Haggerston original, this cool Clerkenwell kebab joint serves shawarma with pride. In Katz’s hands, the iconic late-night eatery offers lamb marinated with the 12-spice house rub and loaded with homemade pickles, s’chug and sumac onions. Elsewhere on the lively Middle Eastern menu, there’s barbecued whole bream with chermoula and burnt lemon and an Iraqi-style hummus pita with amba and egg. restaurants.description: Originally a Haymarket oyster bar opened in the middle of the 19th century, this London institution is now one of Mayfair’s most sought-after tables. With an alleged fish bill of £45,000 per week, English, French and Irish oysters rub shells, while a platter of fruits de mer has justly achieved must-order status. Dover sole, taken from dayboats, is another signature, with south Devon crab and Dublin Bay prawns as highlights for starters, while the roast shellfish platter for two is among the irresistible-sounding mains.

restaurants.description: The legacy of Joël Robuchon lives on at Le Comptoir Robuchon in Mayfair. The velvet-and-marble-clad restaurant has all the buzz of the original Atelier in Paris, with the 20 seats at the counter in high demand. Head chef Fabio Fasano channels the spirit of the great man for la tarte au caviar with king crab and avocado; cheese soufflé, with vin jaune and chorizo bouillon; and le Black Cod with Malabar pepper sauce and coconut mousse. Just looking for a sandwich? Le Deli Robuchon is around the corner.

restaurants.description: Back in 2015, Tomos Parry, grill chef extraordinaire, made Mayfair’s Kitty Fisher’s the hottest restaurant in London. In 2018, he opened this Basque-inspired restaurant in Shoreditch, where he has worked similar magic. Carefully sourced ingredients are allowed to shine with minimal fuss. restaurants.description: When Ruth Rogers and the late Rose Gray opened the River Cafe in 1987, the approach they brought to running a restaurant – quality seasonal Italian ingredients; short, simple menu items written by hand; and most importantly, pride in doing little, rather than more – set an example for countless London restaurants that followed. restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: Meat Liquor has come a long way since it was a food truck in a pub car park. Fifteen years later, it has ten sites and a strong following among the burgerati. The aesthetics are edgy, the burgers dirty, the drinks strong. Big hits include the double patty Dead Hippie, fried in French’s Mustard; fried pickles; and Monkey Fingers slathered in buffalo and blue cheese sauce. Vegans can have a Tempeh Tantrum. Chase with shots, hard shakes or cans of beer.

restaurants.description: In 2008, acclaimed Irish chef Richard Corrigan opened his eponymous restaurant, which focuses on the British Isles’ finest ingredients. Carlingford oysters, Orkney scallops, and Middle White pork are treated with care and attention, paired with classical accompaniments and largely allowed to speak for themselves. Pears from the Loire Valley served with a Sauternes dessert-wine jelly are a standout finish. Just a few steps from Hyde Park allows one to walk off their lunch in a lovely setting.

restaurants.description: As one would expect of a gastropub led by Brett Graham, the Harwood Arms is serious about good food. Game is its raison d’être, and you’ll often find beasts shot by Graham or co-owner Mike Robinson on the menu. Joshua Cutress brings an aristocratic English touch to the kitchen: the venison Scotch egg is a must, followed by Muntjac pâté en croute with port and peppercorn jelly, then beef short rib, confit celeriac and pickled walnuts. You’ll find big-hitting wines and Sunday roasts too.

restaurants.description: It was his then-boss Pierre Koffmann who encouraged French-trained Indian chef Sameer Taneja to try his hand at Indian cooking. It was good advice. For Taneja is now executive chef at Benares, having taken over from founding chef Atul Kochhar in 2018. The smart Berkeley Square spot remains one of London’s premier addresses for modern Indian cooking, with an offering that spans set menu, a bottomless Thali lunch and a tasting menu. The cellar is global, and many bottles pair well with the spice-laden food.

restaurants.description: Angela Hartnett’s chic Mayfair flagship exudes the warmth and sophistication one would expect of this accomplished chef. Her modern Italian menus offer flexibility: from three to six courses of the likes of pumpkin agnolotti, sage butter and amaretti; venison, chestnut gnocchi, chanterelles and chocolate; and Amalfi lemon tart.

restaurants.description: In London Bridge, Chef Jonny Lake and sommelier Isa Bal share equal billing at Trivet, a sleek, serious restaurant where food and wine work in symbiosis. The duo worked together at The Fat Duck and opened Trivet in 2019. Lake’s open kitchen turns out highly original modern European cuisine. Signatures are chicken with a vinegar sauce and a baked potato millefeuille with sake gelato, while Bal’s 350-strong list bucks tradition by categorizing wines historically, starting in Georgia before moving onto Armenia and Turkey

restaurants.description: Way up high on level 33 of The Shard, northern Chinese restaurant Hutong is more than just a spectacular view. It’s the London outpost of the Aqua Restaurant Group’s Hong Kong original, and it pulls off all its sister restaurants’ biggest hits in some style: glossy roasted Peking duck carved tableside, ma la crispy eel, braised chinkiang pork ribs and beautifully crafted lunchtime dim sum platters. restaurants.description: The Soho sequel to the original in Bloomsbury, Noble Rot Soho occupies the site of what was once the Gay Hussar at 2 Greek Street. Popular with the sommelier set – the owners publish Noble Rot magazine – its cellar leans French (Burgundy, Jura, and the Loire) but loves its Piedmont and Rioja wines too. There’s plenty by the glass, and a good range under as well as over £100. Francophile chef Alex Jackson (ex-Sardine) cooks roast chicken, morels and riz au pilaf; Cornish monkfish, Tarbais beans, and roast artichokes; chocolate mousse and brandy prunes.

restaurants.description: From chia bowls to truffled scrambled eggs, chocolate cake to truffle pizza, Jean-Georges at The Connaught gives its soigné guests what they want, when they want it. Celebrity chef Jean-Georges Vongerichten has a vast global empire of restaurants, so he knows exactly how the jet set eats; his crispy salmon sushi and egg toast caviar are always in fashion, as are simple grills of salmon and Cornish lamb chops, and or you can splurge on blue lobster and Wagyu beef. restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: When Ollie Dabbous’s giant, three-floor Piccadilly operation in partnership with Hedonism Wines opened in mid-2018, it immediately gained classic status, thanks to Dabbous’s cooking wizardry. Trained under Raymond Blanc at Le Manoir aux Quat’Saisons, he melds classic French cooking with exciting flavors and quite a lot of dry ice. On the ground floor is the more casual all-day brasserie, while Above, on the first floor, is tasting-menu-only. High-end, high-provenance ingredients meet unexpectedly in the two (think wagyu beef shavings, oscietra caviar and rye in oxtail broth). The bar in the basement, called Below, serves a lengthy list of familiar and exotic spirits.

Current head chef Asimakis Chaniotis blends his Greek heritage into the French cuisine; dishes like salted cod skordalia with crispy shallots, dill and oscietra caviar live in harmony with smoked quail with celeriac, hazelnuts and black truffle. restaurants.address: The Goring Hotel, 15 Beeston Pl, London, United Kingdom
restaurants.description: At The Goring, the hotel that was issued a Royal Warrant by her late Majesty and was Margaret Thatcher’s regular haunt during her tenure in office, it feels tantamount to treason not to order the long-standing classics – the beef Wellington for two, the lobster omelet, or the Queen Mother’s favorite, eggs Drumkilbo. But there is more to the aristocratically appointed dining room than that – for example, rich Aquarello risotto, girolles, confit egg yolk, smoked butter and truffle, or Anjou pigeon with salt-baked golden beetroot, apricot and liver parfait. Tip-top breakfasts too.

restaurants.description: Sushi master Mitsuhiro Araki shuttered his world-renowned Ginza restaurant in 2014 to pursue a new challenge in London. The Araki, a sliver of a sushi-ya in Mayfair, raised London’s sushi game overnight, making headlines with its £300 omakase menu, served to the lucky few (two sittings of nine guests a night). Araki-san handed over the reins to his British apprentice Marty Lau in 2019. Lau upholds his master’s standards, serving edomae sushi piece by piece. High-grade tuna is a speciality, enhanced sometimes by caviar and truffles, and served with rice from the Araki family farm.

restaurants.description: Umu, entering its 20th year, is the only restaurant to have survives the collapse of Marlon Abela’s M.A.R.C group; it now operates independently from its Mayfair mews home. restaurants.description: The cult of Dishoom is a true phenomenon. The Raj-themed, Indian fast-casual restaurant now has six London branches, although the shabbily ornate Shoreditch one, with its cocktail bar and sun terrace, remains the best. Executive chef Naved Nasir, who trained at Delhi’s best hotel restaurant, Bukhara, has been with Dishoom since the first branch opened in 2010. His menu cleverly fuses British staples with Bombay flavors – breakfast naan filled with Shropshire sausage or streaky bacon; creamy black daal; lamb raan buns. Expect a line.

restaurants.description: Quo Vadis, founded in 1926, is a Soho institution. Now in the hands of the Hart brothers (Barrafina, etc.) and much-loved Scottish chef Jeremy Lee, it’s a white-linen and wildflower-bedecked refuge from the world outside. The smoked-eel sandwich is now iconic, but the British seasons have plenty more to give – such as hake, crab and tomato and tarragon broth, pheasant with cabbage and bacon, and “proper” puddings like brown-sugar meringue with Yorkshire rhubarb, cream and custard.

restaurants.description: One of London's most singular restaurants, Otto’s is an exercise in flamboyance, excess and devotion. Canard a la presse – duck carcass crushed at the table – sums up everything about the place: it’s messy, intense, fiercely traditional and worth experiencing at least once. Otto Tepasse is the ideal chef-owner, a ferociously passionate stickler for how he wants things to be married to a generosity of spirit, always encouraging another dessert, glass of excellent French wine or eau de vie.

restaurants.description: Angelo Sato, born and raised in Tokyo, has a résumé bristling with big names (Ryugin, Eleven Madison Park, Restaurant Gordon Ramsay, Restaurant Story …). He took on the former Barrafina site on Frith Street in 2021, reimagining it as a yakitori bar. He reimagined it again two years later, upgrading it into one of the vibiest omakase joints in town. Rare is the table that does not order the beef wing rib for two with crispy potatoes and a bottle of keenly priced old-world wine from the short reserve cellar. restaurants.description: St. Barts, the third restaurant from Johnnie Crowe, Luke Wasserman and Toby Neill, is their most ambitious project to date. The Scandi-style restaurant takes its name from the Church of St. Bartholomew the Great, whose cloisters it overlooks. restaurants.description: In spite of its awards and long-standing success, Kitchen W8 feels like an under-the-radar find. restaurants.description: Chet Sharma swapped his lab coat for chef’s whites when he left Oxford University (where he got his physics PhD) to work at Mugaritz. He’s worked at a veritable Ivy League of big names since, mostly on the R&D side, including The Ledbury, Moor Hall and L’Enclume. BiBi, in Mayfair, launched in 2021, sees him partner with JKS Restaurants (Gymkhana, Sabor, etc.). restaurants.description: Rohit Ghai’s lavishly appointed town house restaurant can be found hidden away down a Chelsea side street, just off the King’s Road. Knock on the discreetly marked front door and step into a world of opulent fabrics, Zoffany wallpaper and costly furnishings inspired by India’s wildlife. restaurants.description: In the carnivorous City of London, Angler stands out with its fish-focused menu. Executive chef Gary Foulkes has worked for the legends of modern British cuisine, among them Gary Rhodes, John Campbell and Philip Howard, and one can detect their influence, quite literally in the case of Phil Howard’s XL Langoustine Dish, with parmesan gnocchi, truffle purée, potato and truffle emulsion. restaurants.description: Henry Harris and his smart French restaurant Racine were Knightsbridge fixtures between 2002 and 2015. Fans of Harris’s unreconstructed brand of bourgeois French cuisine had reason to rejoice in 2022, when Harris resuscitated Racine in Farringdon. Waiters bring the blackboard menu from table to table. Choosing isn’t easy with so many lesser-spotted classics available, including tête de veau with sauce ravigote, rabbit with mustard sauce and smoked bacon, escargots à la bourguignonne or Fredy Girardet–style tarte vaudoise à la crème. restaurants.name: Lady of the Grapes
restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: The aim of Lady of the Grapes, an organic wine bar and restaurant in Covent Garden, is to highlight the female winemakers in a male-dominated industry. On her list of 350+ organic, biodynamic, natural wines, French owner Carole Bryon shines a light on some names you may well know – Fanny Sabre, Elisabetta Foradori, Arianna Occhipinti – as well as many you won’t. The list is fast-moving; at any one time, she’ll have 35 on by the glass, to go with Gallic small plates like whole barbecued quail, celeriac rémoulade or cod cheeks with sauce vierge.

restaurants.priceBasis: Estimated from OAD category; OAD does not publish price bands.
restaurants.description: This is the flagship of Rare Restaurants’ Gaucho Group, an upscale Argentinian-style steakhouse chain with 18 branches across the UK from London to Glasgow. Gaucho’s on a mission to achieve net-zero carbon emissions by 2030, to which end all its beef is 100 percent carbon offset. Choose churrasco (skirt), cuadril (rump) or the day’s wagyu special, and South American dishes like tiraditos, empanadas and dulce de leche cheesecake. Argentine wine is a focus. There’s an all-weather roof terrace for cocktails and cigars.
